title: How Python Runs Your Code
summary: Discover how Python reads and runs your code line by line — and why that makes it one of the friendliest languages to learn.

blocks.md: Before you write your first real program, it helps to understand what actually *happens* when you run Python code. You don't need to memorize any of this — but having a simple mental model will make error messages less scary and debugging much easier.

Python reads your code **from top to bottom**, one line at a time. It does exactly what each line says, then moves on to the next. Think of it like a recipe: a chef follows step 1, then step 2, then step 3 — never skipping ahead, never going back (unless you tell it to).

blocks.code:
print("Step 1: Mix the ingredients")
print("Step 2: Bake for 30 minutes")
print("Step 3: Let it cool")
blocks.output:
Step 1: Mix the ingredients
Step 2: Bake for 30 minutes
Step 3: Let it cool
blocks.caption: Python runs these three lines in order, top to bottom.

blocks.title: Python as a careful reader
blocks.md: Imagine handing someone a list of instructions written on a piece of paper. They read line 1, do it, then read line 2, do it — all the way to the end. Python works exactly the same way.

When you run a Python file, a few quick things happen automatically:

- Python reads your **source code** (the `.py` file you wrote)
- It converts it into a simpler form called **bytecode** (think of it as shorthand Python can work with faster)
- The **Python interpreter** — also called the Python Virtual Machine, or PVM — executes that bytecode

The important part: **you never have to do any of this manually.** You just write your code and hit run. Python handles the rest.

blocks.title: No manual compiling needed
blocks.md: Some languages (like C or Java) require a separate "compile" step before you can run your code. Python handles this automatically — you just run your file and Python figures everything out instantly.
There are two common ways to use Python, and you will encounter both.

**1. Running a script file**

You write your code in a file (e.g. `hello.py`) and run the whole file at once. Python reads it from top to bottom and executes every line. This is how real programs work.

**2. The interactive shell (REPL)**

You open a Python prompt and type one line at a time. Python runs each line immediately and shows you the result. It is perfect for experimenting and learning. REPL stands for **Read, Evaluate, Print, Loop** — it reads what you type, evaluates it, prints the result, then waits for more.

blocks.code:
# This is what the interactive shell looks like.
# The >>> is Python's prompt, waiting for your input.

>>> 2 + 2
4
>>> print("Hello!")
Hello!
blocks.caption: In the interactive shell, you type one line and Python responds right away.

blocks.md: Because Python runs your code line by line, **errors are reported exactly when the problematic line runs**. Any lines before it will have already executed successfully. Any lines after it will not run at all.

blocks.code:
print("This runs fine")
print("This also runs fine")
print(oops_not_a_variable)
print("This never runs")
blocks.output:
This runs fine
This also runs fine
NameError: name 'oops_not_a_variable' is not defined
blocks.caption: Python got through lines 1 and 2, then hit an error on line 3 and stopped.

blocks.title: Code after an error does not run
blocks.md: When Python hits an error, it **stops immediately**. The lines below the error are never executed. This is why fixing errors from top to bottom — one at a time — is the most effective approach.
Because Python reads top to bottom, the **order of your lines matters**. If you try to use something before you have defined it, Python will not know what you are talking about.

blocks.code:
# This works — we define the variable first, then use it.
greeting = "Hello, world!"
print(greeting)
blocks.caption: Define first, use second. Python is happy.

blocks.code:
# This fails — we try to use the variable before defining it.
print(greeting)
greeting = "Hello, world!"
blocks.caption: Python reaches the print line first and has no idea what 'greeting' is yet.

blocks.title: Always define before you use
blocks.md: If you get a `NameError`, the most common cause is using a variable or value **before** the line that creates it. Scroll up and check the order of your code.

blocks.question: You have a Python script with 5 lines. Line 3 has an error. What happens when you run the script?
options: Python skips line 3 and runs lines 4 and 5
Python runs lines 1 and 2, then stops at line 3 with an error
Python finds the error before running anything and refuses to start
Python runs all 5 lines and shows the error at the end
blocks.explanation: Python runs line by line from the top. It successfully executes lines 1 and 2, then hits the error on line 3 and stops. Lines 4 and 5 never run.

keyTakeaways: Python reads and runs your code **top to bottom**, one line at a time.
Python automatically converts your code to bytecode and runs it — no manual compile step needed.
You can run Python as a **script file** (all at once) or in the **interactive shell** (line by line).
When an error occurs, Python stops at that exact line — lines above it already ran, lines below it did not.
**Order matters**: always define a variable before you use it.

exercise.prompt: Write a short Python script (4-5 lines) that does the following in order: (1) prints your name, (2) stores your favorite number in a variable, (3) prints a sentence that includes that number. Make sure the order is correct so everything runs without errors.
exercise.starterCode:
# Step 1: print your name

# Step 2: store your favorite number

# Step 3: print a sentence with that number
exercise.hint: Remember to define the variable before you try to print it. Use print() for each output line.
exercise.solution:
# Step 1: print your name
print("My name is Alex")

# Step 2: store your favorite number
favorite_number = 7

# Step 3: print a sentence with that number
print("My favorite number is", favorite_number)
